_comment: Source: NASEMSO National Model EMS Clinical Guidelines v3.0 (March 2022). Toxins and Environmental: Acetylcholinesterase Inhibitors (Carbamates, Nerve Agents, Organophosphates) Exposure. NASEMSO national provisional base.

condition: Acetylcholinesterase Inhibitors (Carbamates, Nerve Agents, Organophosphates) Exposure
protocol_reference: NASEMSO National Model EMS Clinical Guidelines v3.0 — Toxins and Environmental: Acetylcholinesterase Inhibitors (Carbamates, Nerve Agents, Organophosphates) Exposure

sections.title: Patient Care Goals
sections.reference: NASEMSO National Model EMS Clinical Guidelines v3.0 — Toxins and Environmental: Acetylcholinesterase Inhibitors (Carbamates, Nerve Agents, Organophosphates) Exposure
points: Rapid recognition of the signs and symptoms of confirmed or suspected acetylcholinesterase inhibitor (AChEI) agents such as carbamates, nerve agents, or organophosphates exposure followed by expeditious and repeated administration of atropine, the primary antidote
Carbamates and organophosphates are commonly active agents in commercial insecticides

Accidental carbamate exposure rarely requires treatment 1. DUMBELS is a mnemonic used to describe the signs and symptoms of acetylcholinesterase inhibitor agent poisoning. All patient age groups are included where the signs and symptoms exhibited are consistent with the toxidrome of DUMBELS a. Diarrhea

sections.title: Patient Presentation
points: EXCLUSION CRITERIA:
None noted

sections.title: EMR Interventions
sections.reference: NASEMSO v3.0 — EMR level (Emergency Medical Responder)
points: Decontamination first: remove patient from exposure, remove contaminated clothing (2 layers removes 80% contamination)
Self-protection: don chemical-resistant gloves, gown, eye protection before patient contact
Auto-injector antidotes (atropine + pralidoxime per nerve agent kit): administer per protocol for nerve agent exposure with SLUDGE/DUMBELS signs
BVM ventilation for respiratory failure; suction excessive secretions
CPR/AED if pulseless

sections.title: EMT Interventions
sections.reference: NASEMSO v3.0 — EMT level (Emergency Medical Technician)
points: Mark-1 auto-injector kit (atropine 2 mg + pralidoxime 600 mg IM) for symptomatic nerve agent exposure
Repeat atropine every 5-10 minutes until secretions dry (no upper limit in true nerve agent poisoning)
Albuterol SVN for bronchospasm component
Suction copious secretions; supraglottic airway if needed
12-lead ECG: bradycardia common; monitor for QT prolongation

sections.title: AEMT Interventions
points: Atropine 2-4 mg IV/IO every 5-10 min until secretions controlled (mydriasis is NOT the endpoint)
Pralidoxime (2-PAM) 1-2 g IV over 15-30 min — must be given within 24 hours of exposure for efficacy
IV/IO access; aggressive fluid resuscitation for shock component
Benzodiazepine (midazolam 2-5 mg IV) for seizure from cholinergic crisis
Waveform capnography for monitoring

sections.title: Paramedic Interventions
points: Atropine: no maximum dose — titrate to drying of secretions; doses of 20+ mg may be required in severe poisoning
Pralidoxime (2-PAM) 1-2 g IV over 15-30 min; maintain infusion 500 mg/hour
Diazepam 5-10 mg IV or midazolam 5 mg IV for seizures; aggressive treatment prevents permanent brain damage
RSI and intubation: succinylcholine is CONTRAINDICATED (prolonged paralysis due to AChE inhibition) — use rocuronium 1.2 mg/kg
Glycopyrrolate as atropine alternative for peripheral secretions if central effects not needed
Dopamine or norepinephrine for refractory hypotension
12-lead ECG: treat bradycardia per bradycardia protocol (atropine first line)

sections.title: Patient Safety Considerations
points: Continuous and ongoing patient reassessment is critical
Clinical response to treatment is demonstrated by the drying of secretion and the easing of respiratory effort
Initiation of and ongoing treatment should not be based upon heart rate or

Precautions for pralidoxime chloride administration: a. Although Duodote' and ATNAA' contains atropine, the primary antidote for an acetylcholinesterase inhibitor agent poisoning, the inclusion of pralidoxime chloride in the auto-injector can present challenges if additional doses of atropine are warranted by the patient condition and other formulations of atropine are unavailable: i. Pediatrics: an overdose of pralidoxime chloride may cause profound neuromuscular weakness and subsequent respiratory depression
Considerations during the use of auto-injectors a. If an auto-injector is administered, a dose calculation prior to administration is not necessary

sections.title: Educational Pearls
points: Clinical effects of acetylcholinesterase inhibitor agents a. The clinical effects are caused by the inhibition of the enzyme acetylcholinesterase which allows excess acetylcholine to accumulate in the nervous system
Organophosphates Insecticides a. Can be legally purchased by the general public
Nerve agents a. Traditionally classified as weapons of mass destruction (WMD)

sections.title: Key Documentation
points: ' Time to recognize initial signs and symptoms
' Number of repeated doses of atropine required for the secretions diminish and respirations to improve
' Patient reassessments
' Patient responses to therapeutic interventions
' Measures taken to decontaminate the patient
' Measures taken to protect clean environments from contamination

EMR: Scene safety, primary survey, CPR, AED, BVM, bleeding control, oral airway, O2 therapy, epinephrine auto-injector for anaphylaxis, naloxone unit-dose auto-injector
EMT: EMR scope PLUS: NPA, supraglottic airway, CPAP, pulse oximetry, 12-lead ECG acquisition, oral glucose, aspirin, patient own nitroglycerin, albuterol SVN, IM epinephrine, blood glucose monitoring, cervical collar, traction splint
AEMT: EMT scope PLUS: waveform capnography, IO access, IV access initiation, IV/IO fluids, IV medications (dextrose, naloxone, epinephrine, glucagon, analgesics, antiemetics), subcutaneous/intranasal medications, nitroglycerin from stock
Paramedic: AEMT scope PLUS: ET intubation, RSI, cricothyrotomy, needle decompression, manual defibrillation, cardioversion, transcutaneous pacing, 12-lead ECG interpretation, all antidysrhythmics, vasopressors, all medication routes and classes

deterioration_flags: Continuous and ongoing patient reassessment is critical
scope_notes: EMR scope is the minimum national standard — state/MCA may expand
All interventions subject to local medical director credentialing
Pediatric patients: use weight-based dosing and age-appropriate equipment
Contact medical direction when required by local protocol or for interventions outside standard scope
